condition.qualitative_description: Three or more severe drought weeks in the latest kharif season, or at least one severe week combined with four or more moderate weeks, indicates a drought episode sufficient to cause significant crop water stress and yield loss.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Persistent severe drought weeks during kharif directly reduce soil moisture below crop wilting thresholds. In semi-arid Deccan Plateau settings (AER-6), where rainfed cotton and soybean dominate, even brief severe drought spells during critical growth stages (flowering, grain fill) cause irreversible yield loss. The India Drought Manual uses SPI-based weekly classification to define severe drought; three or more such weeks in a season constitutes a declared agricultural drought in most state protocols.

condition.qualitative_description: Four or more dry spell weeks (4-week moving window with <50% historical rainfall) in the latest kharif season indicate prolonged moisture deficit disrupting crop growth stages.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Dry spells of four or more consecutive weeks during kharif are a primary mechanism of agricultural drought in semi-arid India. In Vidarbha and Marathwada, intra-seasonal dry spells frequently interrupt monsoon rainfall, causing soil moisture depletion in shallow Vertisols and Alfisols. Research on crop water management under drought confirms that unproductive water losses and soil moisture deficits during critical phenological windows are the dominant yield-limiting factors in rainfed systems.

condition.qualitative_description: SPI at or below -1.0 (moderate to severe meteorological drought) combined with VCI below 40 (moderate vegetation stress) confirms co-occurrence of meteorological and agricultural drought in the same kharif season.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: The India Drought Manual prescribes a composite drought classification using SPI for meteorological drought, MAI for soil moisture adequacy, and VCI for vegetation condition. Studies on kharif rice in Indian districts show that SPI and VCI are both significantly positively correlated with crop productivity index, with combined use explaining 69–76% of yield variability. An SPI ≤ -1.0 with VCI < 40 in the same season provides strong confirmation that rainfall deficit has translated into crop-level water stress, distinguishing true drought from localised irrigation failure.

condition.qualitative_description: Kharif seasonal rainfall below 75% of the long-run annual mean, or a declining multi-year precipitation trend below 700 mm/year, confirms structural rainfall deficit underpinning drought risk.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: In AER-6 (Deccan Plateau, semi-arid), mean annual rainfall ranges from 500–960 mm with high inter-annual variability. Below-normal kharif rainfall (less than 75% of the long-run mean) is the primary trigger for agricultural drought in rainfed systems. A declining precipitation trend alongside a low baseline (<700 mm) signals increasing structural drought risk. Water-food-energy nexus analyses for India confirm that after drought years, groundwater storage drops and cannot recover even in subsequent good monsoon years, amplifying multi-year drought impacts.

condition.qualitative_description: Monsoon onset in the latest year is more than 7 days later than the earliest year in the record, indicating a trend toward delayed onset that shortens the effective kharif growing season.

diagnostic_signals.explanation: Delayed monsoon onset in semi-arid Deccan regions shortens the effective kharif season, compressing the window for sowing and early crop establishment. A delay of more than one week relative to historical norms forces farmers to sow into drier soils or miss optimal sowing windows entirely, increasing vulnerability to subsequent dry spells. Crop ecology research confirms that synchronisation between crop water demand and soil moisture supply is critical, and delayed onset disrupts this synchronisation for rainfed cotton and soybean.

confounders.confounder: Groundwater depletion causing apparent drought-like water scarcity even in normal rainfall years
confounders.how_to_distinguish: Check annual_precipitation_mm and seasonal_precipitation_mm against historical baseline. If rainfall is near-normal but farmers still report water scarcity, the pathway is more likely groundwater stress than meteorological drought. Confirm by examining drought_weeks_severe and drought_causality_json SPI values — if SPI is near zero but water stress persists, groundwater depletion is the primary driver. In hard rock aquifers of Vidarbha and Telangana, borewell failure during normal rainfall years is a known indicator of over-extraction rather than drought.

confounders.confounder: Irrigation infrastructure failure (canal or tank breach) mimicking drought water scarcity for rabi and zaid crops
confounders.how_to_distinguish: Drought pathway primarily affects kharif rainfed crops through rainfall deficit; irrigation failure affects rabi and zaid crops even when kharif rainfall was adequate. Check seasonal_precipitation_mm for kharif — if kharif rainfall was adequate but rabi water scarcity is reported, investigate tank or canal infrastructure status rather than confirming drought. Drought_weeks_severe and dry_spell_weeks are kharif-season indicators and will not flag rabi irrigation failure.

sources.title: Management of crop water under drought: a review
sources.relevance_note: Reviews crop water management under drought, confirming that unproductive losses and soil moisture deficits during critical phenological windows are dominant yield-limiting factors in rainfed semi-arid systems; supports dry spell and severe drought week thresholds.

sources.title: Drought assessment for kharif rice using standardized precipitation index (SPI) and vegetation condition index (VCI)
sources.relevance_note: Demonstrates that SPI and VCI are both significantly positively correlated with kharif crop productivity index in Indian districts; combined use explains 69–76% of yield variability, supporting the composite SPI+VCI signal in sig_03.

sources.title: Water–food–energy nexus with changing agricultural scenarios in India during recent decades
sources.relevance_note: Documents that after drought years in India, groundwater storage drops and cannot recover even in subsequent good monsoon years; supports the multi-year precipitation trend signal and the interaction between drought and groundwater stress.

sources.title: Drought Management Manual
authors: Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare
sources.relevance_note: Defines India's official drought classification framework using SPI, MAI, and VCI; prescribes thresholds for meteorological and agricultural drought declaration used in sig_01 and sig_03.

overall_reasoning_note: Drought as a causal pathway for water scarcity in semi-arid Deccan agriculture should be confirmed by at least two of the three primary signals: persistent severe or moderate drought weeks (sig_01), extended dry spells (sig_02), or composite SPI+VCI confirmation (sig_03). Seasonal and annual precipitation signals (sig_04) provide structural context, while delayed monsoon onset (sig_05) amplifies but does not independently confirm drought. A single signal may reflect data noise or spatial mismatch. The key distinction from groundwater stress is that drought signals are grounded in rainfall deficit metrics — if SPI is near zero and drought weeks are low but water scarcity persists, redirect diagnosis toward groundwater depletion. Always verify whether reported water scarcity is in kharif (rainfed, drought-driven) or rabi/zaid (irrigation-dependent, potentially infrastructure-driven) before finalising the pathway.
